title: What to Look Out for in Economic Data This Week (April 27-May 1)
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: Watch for the FOMC decision, Q1 GDP release, and major tech earnings. Inflation and Middle East tensions remain key factors.

content.introduction: Watch for the FOMC decision, Q1 GDP release, and major tech earnings. Inflation and Middle East tensions remain key factors. Inflation data for Australia is expected to run hot with the Middle East tensions remaining the main driver.
body:
## Market Overview

In short, Q1 likely marks the beginning of a renewed inflation push rather than the peak and markets are looking for firmer price pressures ahead. Markets will focus on policymakers assessment of the recent pickup in prices, with headline inflation likely to move back above the 1-3% target band in April, driven by higher energy costs. ING analysts expect the BoC to keep rates steady through 2026, though the market is generally pricing a 25bps hike by year's end. As in other regions, higher energy costs in Europe are expected to push up headline inflation. The Bank is likely to highlight the impact of the energy shock, which is contributing to higher inflation alongside weaker growth. The report will be published after the BoJ meeting, and the Bank is expected to closely monitor it for confirmation that price pressures remain sticky. Jerome Powell will take the lead for what is presumably his final FOMC meeting and press conference as Fed chair this week. US macro data: Q1 GDP, Core PCE, and labour market data will provide key signals on growth and inflation dynamics. Oil market developments: with the best of Kiplinger's advice on investing, taxes, retirement, personal finance and much more. with the best of Kiplinger's advice on investing, taxes, retirement, personal finance and much more . Watch for the FOMC decision, Q1 GDP release, and major tech earnings. Inflation and Middle East tensions remain key factors. Inflation data for Australia is expected to run hot with the Middle East tensions remaining the main driver. Even though the effects were limited in the March data, it will be different for this month's figures and the impulse is still building. The impact will likely be more widespread in the June quarter and over the remainder of the year. Westpac analysts stress that underlying inflation is also holding firm. Trimmed mean CPI is projected near 0.9% q q, keeping the annual pace in the mid-3% range. While core measures tend to react more gradually, they re not insulated from rising energy costs, and momentum is expected to strengthen through the second half of the year. In short, Q1 likely marks the beginning of a renewed inflation push rather than the peak and markets are looking for firmer price pressures ahead. The BoC is widely expected to keep rates on hold at this week s meeting as it balances rising inflation against still-manageable economic conditions. Thursday brings a heavy slate of U.S. data, starting with advance GDP q q, where the consensus is 2.2% versus 0.5% previously. The core PCE price index m m is expected at 0.3% compared to 0.4% prior. The main focus is on Q1 GDP, following a softer 0.5% annualized expansion in Q4 that was distorted by the October government shutdown. That drag is expected to reverse at the start of the year, with public spending rebounding and supporting overall activity.

## Key Developments

Beneath the surface, however, the growth mix appears uneven. Technology investment remains a key driver and continues to expand strongly, while net trade is expected to be broadly neutral. Fiscal stimulus, dollar weakness and regional catalysts should support strong international performance. This weekly update provides a snapshot of changes in the economy and markets and their implications for investors. Originally Posted April 27, 2026 Economic Update The Market Insights program provides comprehensive data and commentary on global markets without reference to products. Designed as a tool to help clients understand the markets and support investment decision-making, the program explores the implications of current economic data and changing market conditions. The March Jobs report came in stronger than expected, with the U.S. That said, gains in the prior two months were revised down by 7k, bringing the three-month moving average of payroll gains to 68k. In the details, both goods and services sectors added jobs, with construction ( 26k) leading the charge in the former and heath care and social assistance ( 90k) leading the latter. Elsewhere, the unemployment rate fell to 4.3% due to a 396k decline in the labor force as the labor force participation rate ticked down to 61.9%. Wage growth eased to 0.2% m m and 3.5% y y, suggesting very little additional inflationary pressures coming from the labor market. Overall, this report shows a labor market that remains tight, allowing the Fed to remain on hold for most, if not ... ## What Investors Should Watch

Key Economic Data Dropping This Week Tuesday brings Consumer Confidence and the Richmond Fed Manufacturing Survey. Thursday is the heavyweight day, with Initial Jobless Claims, the Employment Cost Index, the first GDP release for Q1, and the Personal Income & PCE Deflator the Fed's preferred inflation gauge all out at 8:30 AM. The Big Tech Earnings Bonanza This is arguably the most important earnings week of the year. Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, and Meta all report on Wednesday, followed by Apple on Thursday. With Tesla already done, only Nvidia will be left to report later in the calendar. Wakamiru Wakamiru's Post Wakamiru Wakamiru 4d This week in finance: April 27 May 1, 2026 1. The Big Macro Event FOMC Decision (Wednesday, April 29) The Federal Open Market Committee announces its interest rate decision on Wednesday, April 29. The Fed has held rates at 3.50 3.75% and signalled one cut for 2026, though a growing minority sees no cuts at all. All eyes will be on Chair Powell's language around inflation and the Middle East conflict. Key Economic Data Dropping This Week ... Wednesday is the collision course: four of the world's largest companies report on the same evening as the FOMC decision meaning markets will digest the Fed's communication before the earnings hit. The shared theme across all four is AI capital expenditure and whether it's producing enough revenue growth to justify the spend. Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, and Microsoft have each rallied more than 10% this month heading into their prints, while Apple has jumped over 6%. Payments & Energy Giants Visa and Verizon report Monday, while T-Mobile and Mastercard follow Tuesday and Thursday respectively. Energy supermajors Exxon Mobil and Chevron, alongside BP, Phillips 66, and Valero, will provide a crucial read on how the Iran war is hitting the energy market. Watch for Jerome Powell's final FOMC meeting, Q1 GDP, Core PCE, and labor market data for growth and inflation signals. Inflation data for Australia is expected to run hot with the Middle East tensions remaining the main driver. The quarter-on-quarter (annualised) GDP figure is the broadest indicator of economic growth. Market participants will continue to monitor developments closely for signals about the direction of economic indicators trends and their broader implications for portfolio allocation and risk management strategies.
